annotation: The model treated the filer as a qualifying surviving spouse without a dependent child, incorrectly used joint Social Security thresholds and deductions, and consequently included only $11,542 of Social Security. The single-filer calculation includes $18,542.70 and uses $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model first included nontaxable veterans benefits and treated ESI premiums as an AGI deduction, then asserted without calculation that deductions eliminated tax despite its own taxable-income estimates exceeding $60,000. Veterans benefits are excluded, while the correct deductions leave $26,095.78 taxable and $2,883.49 of tax.

The model incorrectly treated 85% of Social Security as automatically taxable once provisional income exceeded $34,000; the statutory worksheet yields $18,542.70, not $22,182. It also chose the aged standard deduction instead of the larger $18,341.33 itemized deduction before applying the separate $6,000 senior deduction.

The model assigned qualifying-surviving-spouse status without the required dependent child and used joint thresholds and deductions. It then declared approximately $14,000 of remaining taxable income to be zero, whereas the single-filer computation leaves $26,095.78 taxable.

The model used qualifying-surviving-spouse status and joint brackets without a dependent child, and treated 85% of Social Security as taxable rather than applying the single-filer worksheet amount of $18,542.70. It also omitted the separate $6,000 senior deduction and failed to use the $18,341.33 itemized deduction.

The model's stated inputs produce about $2,023 of tax, but it replaced that result with $3,204 through a nonexistent bracket adjustment. It also used qualifying-surviving-spouse rules, included $22,182 rather than $18,542.70 of Social Security, and missed the correct itemized-deduction computation.

The model incorrectly included the full 85% cap of $22,182 as taxable Social Security instead of the worksheet result of $18,542.70. It also used only the aged standard deduction, omitting both the larger $18,341.33 itemized deduction and the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model incorrectly assumed a 2026 TCJA sunset, created a personal exemption, and used pre-TCJA brackets. It also treated the listed employer-sponsored premiums as deductible medical spending; the correct deductions are $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The claimed $37,990 of taxable income cannot produce $1,109 under the applicable 2026 single brackets. The trace instead yields $26,095.78 of taxable income and $2,883.49 after applying the 10% and 12% brackets.

The model invented a 2026 personal exemption and applied pre-TCJA 10% and 15% brackets. It omitted the separate $6,000 senior deduction and understated total itemized deductions at $16,470 rather than $18,341.33.

The model incorrectly reverted to pre-TCJA rules, including a personal exemption and 15% bracket. It used only the stated property tax as itemized deductions and omitted both the full $18,341.33 itemized total and the separate $6,000 senior deduction.

The model subtracted an inapplicable personal exemption and only $16,470 of property tax. The correct calculation subtracts $18,341.33 of itemized deductions and the $6,000 senior deduction, leaving $26,095.78 taxable.

Although the model correctly used single status and approximately the correct taxable Social Security, it capped the property-tax deduction at $10,000 and therefore chose the standard deduction. The applicable itemized deductions total $18,341.33 and exceed the standard deduction; the separate $6,000 senior deduction then reduces taxable income to $26,095.78.

The model asserted that age-related and surviving-spouse deductions eliminated taxable income without computing them. The applicable itemized and senior deductions total $24,341.33 against $50,437.11 of AGI, leaving $26,095.78 taxable rather than zero.

The model incorrectly claimed the aged-filer deduction offsets all taxable pension, IRA, interest, and taxable Social Security income. The correct deductions leave $26,095.78 taxable and therefore do not eliminate federal tax.

The model improperly used qualifying-surviving-spouse filing rules and joint Social Security thresholds, reducing taxable Social Security to $11,542 and granting a joint standard deduction. Single-filer rules produce $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security and use $18,341.33 of itemized deductions plus the $6,000 senior deduction.

The model used an age-based standard deduction rather than the larger $18,341.33 itemized deduction. That deduction choice, together with its $49.30 overstatement of taxable Social Security, left taxable income $240.22 too high and tax $28.85 above the traced result.

The model correctly approximated AGI and the senior deduction but used an $18,150 aged standard deduction instead of $18,341.33 in itemized deductions. This overstated taxable income by about $190 and produced $2,906.32 rather than $2,883.49.

The model's roughly $359 result reflects the same joint-threshold and joint-deduction shortcut used for a qualifying surviving spouse. Without a dependent child, single rules apply, producing $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security and $26,095.78 of taxable income.

The model asserted that senior deductions and credits exceed taxable income without calculating either. The available itemized and senior deductions total $24,341.33, leaving $26,095.78 taxable, and no nonrefundable credit eliminates the resulting tax.

The model incorrectly applied a personal exemption and pre-TCJA 10% and 15% brackets while omitting the $6,000 senior deduction. The correct calculation uses $18,341.33 of itemized deductions, the senior deduction, and the applicable 2026 10% and 12% single brackets.

The model invented a medical deduction from employer-sponsored insurance premiums and applied expired-law 10% and 15% brackets. The correct itemized deduction is $18,341.33, followed by the separate $6,000 senior deduction and current 2026 brackets.
The model supplied no numeric output or explanation for the requested variable, so its response failed the required output contract.

The model used an $18,150 aged standard deduction because it compared itemization only with the stated $16,470 property tax. The traced itemized total is $18,341.33, which is larger; using it with the $6,000 senior deduction lowers taxable income to $26,095.78.

The model subtracted a $750 elderly credit even though its own reasoning recognized that credit as phased out, and it invoked an inapplicable lifetime-learning credit. It also asserted that deductions wipe out income despite estimating substantial taxable income; no credit eliminates the $2,883.49 liability.

The model excluded all Social Security by ignoring that tax-exempt interest and half of Social Security enter provisional income. Provisional income is $50,520, which produces $18,542.70 of taxable Social Security under single-filer rules; the model also improperly used a qualifying-surviving-spouse standard deduction.
All wrong responses were missing or unparseable predictions.

The model correctly stated that no earned wages were provided and that Social Security benefits are not employee-payroll-taxable, but then submitted $1,534 instead of the zero implied by its own rule. Its numeric answer is consistent with applying payroll tax to a nonwage retirement-income component rather than limiting the tax base to listed wages and covered earned compensation.

The model did not provide a parseable payroll_tax value or explanation. The correct derivation uses the listed income categories and finds no wage, salary, or covered earned income, leaving the employee payroll tax base at zero.

The model applied a projected post-TCJA joint standard deduction plus personal exemptions totaling only $28,350, leaving $3,052.25 of taxable income. PolicyEngine's 2026 joint standard deduction exceeds the household's $31,402.21 AGI, so the model created taxable income by using the wrong deduction regime.

The model used a $29,600 deduction amount made from a post-TCJA-style standard deduction and personal exemptions, which left $1,802.25 of taxable income. The correct computation applies the 2026 PolicyEngine standard deduction for joint filers, which is larger than the $31,402.21 AGI and reduces taxable income to zero.

The model overstated taxable Social Security by treating $11,588 of benefits as taxable instead of the $2,338 included in PolicyEngine's AGI, inflating AGI to $40,652. It also used a much too low post-TCJA-style senior standard deduction of about $20,400, while PolicyEngine's 2026 joint standard deduction eliminates taxable income entirely.

The model incorrectly converted age 65+ into ABD Medicaid eligibility and then relied on medically needy or Medicare Savings Program concepts that are not the eligibility category PolicyEngine assigned. It also misapplied SSI-related counting by treating Social Security as largely excluded and concluding the couple passed the aged pathway, while the engine trace shows the SSI pathway fails both income and asset tests and the category is NONE.

The model treated being 71 as making the head potentially Medicaid eligible without applying any Virginia pathway test. The correct derivation checks actual categories: MAGI categories fail at 2.05 FPL, the SSI-related aged pathway fails, and no other category is identified.

The model incorrectly treated Medicare eligibility plus moderate income as qualifying the head through a Medicare Savings Program Medicaid pathway. PolicyEngine's category computation assigns NONE because the head is above the relevant MAGI limits and does not satisfy the SSI-related income and asset tests.

The model correctly computed the two employee FICA components as $1,801.968 and $421.428 but submitted $4,412.328 instead of their sum. It added an extra amount inconsistent with its own stated no-spouse-wages and no-Additional-Medicare-Tax reasoning, effectively doubling the employee-side payroll tax calculation.

The model identified the right tax base and included Social Security plus Medicare with no Additional Medicare Tax, but it did not apply the full 7.65% employee FICA rate to $29,064. The correct computation is $1,801.97 of Social Security tax plus $421.43 of Medicare tax, while $2,090 is an undercalculated shortcut rather than the required component sum.

The model used the right 7.65% employee FICA rate and the right $29,064 wage base, but it rounded the result to $2,220 instead of carrying the payroll tax calculation to dollars and cents. PolicyEngine computes the Social Security and Medicare components separately and rounds to $1,801.97 + $421.43 = $2,223.39.

The model bypassed PolicyEngine's gross-income eligibility screen and treated the elderly household as qualifying under a net-income calculation after deductions. It also converted a mortgage balance into a shelter deduction even though no mortgage payment, rent, property tax, or utility expense was listed, producing an invented positive monthly benefit instead of applying the gross-income cutoff that yields $0.

The model invented an aged pathway based on the spouse's lack of own earnings and ignored that Medicaid eligibility is not determined on separate spousal income alone. It also contradicted its own MAGI calculation: the household's MAGI exceeds the expansion threshold, and the spouse has no SSI-related categorical route in the engine.

The model misapplied Virginia ABD Medicaid by treating age 65+ plus modest assets as enough for eligibility. PolicyEngine did not place the spouse in an ABD, SSI, blind, disabled, dependent, or MAGI Medicaid category, and the household MAGI is 2.05 x FPL, above the MAGI-based limits.

The model used a generic shortcut that a 69-year-old is potentially Medicaid eligible without applying any Virginia pathway. The correct computation requires an actual eligibility category, and the spouse qualifies through none of the MAGI or non-MAGI pathways.

The model confused Medicare age with Medicaid eligibility and asserted Medicare Savings Program eligibility without checking the applicable category and income test. PolicyEngine assigns the spouse no Medicaid category, and the household's income level of 2.05 x FPL blocks MAGI-based Medicaid eligibility.

The model added an unlisted 40-quarter work-history confirmation requirement to the PolicyEngine Medicare eligibility variable. Under the reference rule, the spouse's age 69 alone satisfies Medicare eligibility, so treating missing work credits or Social Security receipt as disqualifying produced the wrong 0 answer.

The model allowed only $21,484 of total Virginia deductions and exemptions, omitting part of the deduction stack available to two spouses over age 65. After excluding Social Security and applying both age deductions, the joint standard deduction, and both personal exemptions, no Virginia taxable income remains.

The model’s unexplained $560 liability implies that it left positive Virginia taxable income after applying only a partial set of deductions. The two age deductions, joint standard deduction, personal exemptions, and Virginia Social Security subtraction eliminate taxable income.

The model incorrectly reduced the head’s Virginia age deduction by Social Security benefits and therefore allowed only the spouse’s $12,000 age deduction. Social Security is subtracted from Virginia income; it does not offset the head’s age deduction, so both qualifying age deductions apply and eliminate taxable income with the other deductions and exemptions.

The model said it applied the Social Security exclusion, both age deductions, and both personal exemptions but produced a positive tax inconsistent with that deduction stack. Those adjustments together with the joint standard deduction reduce Virginia taxable income to zero, leaving no amount to apply the tax brackets to.

The model treated the full $15,225 of Social Security as Virginia income, used conflicting estimated standard deductions, and submitted a number that does not match any of its own bracket calculations. Virginia excludes Social Security, and the resulting wage-based income is fully offset by both age deductions, the joint standard deduction, and personal exemptions.

The model applied single-filer Social Security thresholds, a single-filer standard deduction, and single-filer brackets even though the household is a joint return. Under the married-joint Social Security calculation, only $4,390 of Social Security is taxable, and the joint deduction with two senior additions eliminates all taxable income.

The model improperly included the $24,000 disability benefit in AGI, overstated taxable Social Security at $23,663, and used an obsolete deduction scheme combining a $15,650 standard deduction with personal exemptions. The correct AGI is $23,130, which is fully absorbed by the 2026 joint standard deduction and two senior additions.

The model treated the $24,000 disability benefit as taxable and overstated taxable Social Security at $23,663. Excluding the disability benefit and applying the joint Social Security formula yields $23,130 of AGI, below the applicable joint deduction for two spouses over 65.

The model treated all $44,080 of Social Security as taxable instead of applying the married-joint provisional-income formula. Only $4,390 of Social Security enters AGI, so AGI is $23,130 and the senior-adjusted joint standard deduction leaves no taxable income.

The model asserted $22,203 of taxable income after deductions, which requires overstating taxable income sources or understating the joint deduction. The correct income inclusion is $18,340 of pension, $400 of interest, and $4,390 of taxable Social Security, while the $24,000 disability benefit is excluded; the resulting $23,130 AGI is entirely deducted.

The model expressly included disability income and taxable Social Security in a positive joint taxable-income calculation. The disability benefit is excluded, only $4,390 of Social Security is taxable, and the joint standard deduction plus both age additions reduces the resulting $23,130 AGI to zero taxable income.

The model improperly included the $24,000 disability benefit in AGI and calculated $37,468 of taxable Social Security from a provisional-income base containing that excluded benefit. The correct joint calculation includes only $4,390 of Social Security, producing $23,130 of AGI that is fully offset by the deduction for a joint return with both spouses over 65.

Although the model ultimately excluded the disability benefit, it treated all $44,080 of Social Security as taxable merely because combined income crossed the joint threshold. Crossing the threshold does not make every dollar taxable: the joint formula yields $4,390 of taxable Social Security, and the resulting $23,130 AGI is below the senior-adjusted joint standard deduction.

The model correctly stated that no wages were provided and that Social Security retirement is not subject to payroll tax, then contradicted that rule by imposing $1,926 on unspecified wage-equivalent income. The prompt requires unlisted numeric inputs to be treated as zero, so there is no taxable payroll base for Social Security, Medicare, Additional Medicare Tax, or Washington employee payroll taxes.

The model treated age 75 plus no own income as sufficient for an aged Medicaid pathway, but PolicyEngine assigned the spouse medicaid_category = NONE. It also ignored the household MAGI income level of 2.90 FPL for MAGI categories and substituted an individual-income shortcut that the reference trace does not use for eligibility.

The model omitted the deductible $1,118 traditional IRA contribution, leaving AGI about $1,118 too high, and used estimated rather than actual 2026 brackets. Its submitted $27,049 also contradicts its own stated calculation of roughly $22,379.

The model taxed only $23,384 of Social Security instead of $50,583.25 and incorrectly deducted the nonexistent $23,926 traditional 401(k) deferral despite zero wages. It also assigned an age-65 additional standard deduction even though both spouses are under 65 and invented unidentified nonrefundable credits.

The model incorrectly denied the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction and therefore overstated AGI and taxable income. Its submitted $19,524 also abandons its own final derivation of about $22,652 without identifying any credit or adjustment that produces the reduction.

The model incorrectly rejected the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction, producing AGI of $183,291 instead of $182,172.27. It then used estimated bracket thresholds rather than the applicable 2026 married-filing-jointly brackets.

The model denied the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction and used a $30,000 estimated standard deduction instead of $32,200. Those errors raised its taxable income by about $3,318 before it applied estimated bracket thresholds.

The model included the IRA deduction but misstated pre-deduction income by $100 and rounded the tax to $22,000 instead of completing the bracket calculation. With AGI of $182,172.27 and taxable income of $149,972.27, the ordinary and qualified-dividend taxes total $22,154.70.

The model applied a hypothetical post-TCJA sunset regime with personal exemptions, a $15,000 standard deduction, and 10%/15%/25% brackets. The applicable 2026 rules instead provide a $32,200 joint standard deduction and the current 10%/12%/22% brackets at this income.

The model incorrectly substituted a $16,000 standard deduction plus personal exemptions and applied pre-TCJA 10%/15%/25% rates. The applicable calculation uses a $32,200 standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current 2026 brackets.

The model treated approximately $183,865 as taxable income even after claiming to account for deductions. The correct deductions reduce $184,872.25 of gross income first to $182,172.27 of AGI and then to $149,972.27 of taxable income.

The model assumed TCJA expiration and used personal exemptions and pre-TCJA tax parameters. The applicable 2026 computation uses the $32,200 joint standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current-law brackets.

The model applied a post-TCJA-sunset $16,100 standard deduction, personal exemptions, and pre-TCJA rates. The applicable rules use a $32,200 standard deduction with no personal exemptions and produce $149,972.27 of taxable income.
The model omitted the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction and used a $30,800 standard deduction instead of $32,200. This overstated taxable income by $2,518.23 before applying the brackets.

The model used the wrong capital-loss amount, omitted tax-exempt interest from provisional income, used an unsupported $30,000 deduction, and invented a $500 sick-spouse credit. Its submitted $49,523.45 also bears no relation to its own stated final tax of $28,867.

The model incorrectly treated age-based and itemized deductions as sufficient to eliminate tax. Neither spouse is 65, medical expenses do not exceed the 7.5%-of-AGI floor, and the $32,200 standard deduction leaves $149,972.27 taxable.

The model reduced the liability to $2,320 through unspecified medical and retirement deductions. Medical expenses are below the 7.5%-of-AGI floor, and the valid deductions leave $149,972.27 of taxable income and $22,154.70 of tax.

The model used taxable income of about $151,091, which is $1,118.73 too high because it omitted the deductible traditional IRA contribution. The correct taxable income is $149,972.27 before separating the $3,760 preferential-rate portion.

The model's $22,400.71 result is consistent with omitting the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction and taxing about $151,090.50 rather than $149,972.27. Applying that deduction before the standard deduction lowers the ordinary-income tax to $21,590.70.
The model used taxable income of about $151,091 because it omitted the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction. The correct taxable income is $149,972.27, including $3,760 taxed preferentially.
The model's $151,090.50 taxable income omits the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction. Including it produces $149,972.27 of taxable income and reduces the tax to $22,154.70.

The model incorrectly treated retirement contributions and deductions as eliminating tax on substantial taxable retirement income. The valid above-the-line and standard deductions leave $149,972.27 of taxable income, not zero.

The model assumed post-TCJA-sunset personal exemptions, a reduced standard deduction, and pre-TCJA rates. The applicable 2026 regime has a $32,200 joint standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current brackets.

The model used inflation-adjusted pre-TCJA standard deductions, personal exemptions, and ordinary brackets. The applicable calculation instead uses the $32,200 joint standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current 2026 rates.

The model projected a pre-TCJA regime with a $16,950 standard deduction, two personal exemptions, and 10%/15%/25% brackets. The applicable 2026 rules use a $32,200 standard deduction, no personal exemptions, and current brackets.

The model incorrectly denied the $1,118 traditional IRA deduction and instead deducted $500 of auto-loan interest. The trace's valid above-the-line deductions total $2,699.98, producing $149,972.27 of taxable income after the $32,200 standard deduction.

The model treated the absence of wages as preventing a tax calculation and returned zero. The listed IRA distributions, pension, taxable Social Security, interest, and dividends independently create $149,972.27 of taxable income after deductions.

The model failed to subtract the $2,699.98 of above-the-line deductions correctly, used an age-based deduction despite both spouses being under 65, and overstated taxable income. Its submitted $41,228.50 also contradicts its own stated calculation of $25,531.98.

The model replaced the actual Medicare age test with a near-term eligibility concept. Age 64 does not satisfy the age-65 Medicare threshold for the modeled year, and no non-age Medicare pathway is listed.

The model stated the correct rule that age 64 is below the Medicare eligibility age of 65, then submitted the opposite binary value. Its error is a parse/consistency failure within the substantive answer: the explanation supports not eligible, while the submitted value marks eligible.

The model projected Medicare eligibility from the future age-65 threshold instead of applying eligibility to the constant full-year age facts. The head remains age 64 in the 2026 facts, so the standard age pathway is not met and no alternative Medicare pathway is present.

The model invented W-2 wages from weekly hours and an assumed $10 hourly wage despite the prompt saying unlisted numeric inputs are 0. It then applied Social Security and Medicare tax to those fabricated wages, and its arithmetic also double-counted an unexplained $947.17 component after already computing FICA on the same estimated wage base.

The model annualized labor earnings from hours worked even though no hourly wage or wage amount was provided and unlisted numeric inputs are 0. Payroll tax applies only to actual taxable employee earnings, so applying 6.2% Social Security and 1.45% Medicare rates to inferred wages produced a nonzero liability where the wage base is $0.

The model correctly identified that Texas has no general state individual income tax, then overrode that rule by adding an unsupported $280 residual for a nonstandard state component. The requested variable excludes local income and payroll taxes, and Texas contributes $0 to state income tax before refundable credits for this household.

The model put the full $51,475 Social Security benefit into AGI as if it were ordinary taxable income, then applied the standard deduction to that inflated AGI. It also contradicted itself on the tax calculation, moving from about $2,787 to $3,280 to a submitted $5,885 without a valid credit or surtax step.

The model correctly identified that taxable income is $0 after excluding Social Security under the provisional-income test and applying the standard deduction, then abandoned that computation. Its submitted $4,600 comes from inventing unlisted wage income from the 40-hour work facts, even though the prompt says unlisted numeric inputs are 0 and no wages are listed.

The submitted $3,144 reflects taxing a positive amount of Social Security-related income after a standard deduction rather than applying the provisional-income exclusion. With provisional income below the married-filing-jointly threshold, none of the Social Security benefit is taxable and the remaining $1,065 of non-Social-Security income is below the standard deduction.

The model stated the correct Social Security rule, then computed taxable income as $21,590 by effectively subtracting the standard deduction from gross income that still included Social Security. Once Social Security is excluded from AGI, only $1,065 remains before the standard deduction, so taxable income and pre-refundable-credit federal income tax are both $0.

The model computed MAGI as $1,065 by effectively ignoring the head's $51,475 of Social Security retirement income and taxable interest, then compared that understated income to the 138% FPL adult expansion limit. The correct engine trace has MAGI at 2.39 times FPL, so New York's expansion Medicaid pathway is income-ineligible and no other Medicaid category applies.

The model overrode the age-65 Medicare rule with an unsupported appeal to unspecified “programs” and “internal logic.” The prompt lists age 62 and no Medicare-qualifying disability, ESRD, ALS, or other qualifying status, so the head fails the Medicare eligibility test.

The model's reasoning states the correct Medicare result: age 62 is below the standard eligibility age and the expected modeled value is 0. It then submitted value = 1, making the final parsed answer contradict its own explanation rather than reflecting a substantive eligibility calculation.

The model treated Social Security retirement receipt at age 62 as conferring Medicare eligibility. Early Social Security retirement benefits do not make a person Medicare eligible before age 65 absent a qualifying disability, ESRD, ALS, or similar pathway, none of which is listed for this head.

The model's explanation correctly states that unlisted wages are zero and that Social Security retirement income produces no employee payroll tax, which supports payroll_tax = 0. It then submitted 5108 anyway, so the failure is an internal output/explanation mismatch rather than a substantive tax-rule calculation.

The model invented a very low spouse MAGI of $1,065 and treated that as below 138% FPL for a two-person household. The engine's MAGI calculation is 2.39 times FPL, so the spouse fails the ACA expansion adult income test and has no other Medicaid category.

The model treated most of the head's Social Security benefits as taxable income for New York, even though New York excludes Social Security retirement benefits from state taxable income. It also applied a contradictory bracket calculation to only $4,121 of claimed taxable income, producing a tax far above what New York rates can generate on that base.

The model correctly noted that New York excludes Social Security and that listed non-excluded income is near zero after deductions, then abandoned the prompt's rule that unlisted numeric inputs are zero by inventing wages from full-time hours. Its $3,200 answer comes from applying New York rates to an assumed wage-based income base rather than the household's listed income.

The model gave no usable computation and returned a positive New York income tax despite the listed New York taxable income being eliminated by the married-filing-joint standard deduction and exemptions. Its answer is consistent with applying NY rates to a fabricated positive taxable-income amount rather than excluding Social Security and treating unlisted wages as zero.

The model included the full Social Security retirement benefit in federal AGI for the New York taxable-income build-up and then produced a positive New York taxable income despite acknowledging Social Security is not taxed by New York. It also treated traditional IRA contributions as an add-back and applied brackets to an overstated taxable-income base, yielding a nonzero state tax where the New York base is fully eliminated.

The model deducted the full $46,308 of desired traditional 401(k) contributions and reduced the net capital loss to a $3,000 gross-income deduction, producing the wrong income base. It also omitted the $5,721.98 net investment income tax entirely.

The model invented approximately $110,000 of mortgage interest even though no mortgage-interest expense was listed, then reported a tax amount incompatible with its own approximately $408,000 taxable-income estimate. Employer-sponsored insurance premiums are not nonrefundable income-tax credits, contrary to its stated treatment.

The model explicitly excluded NIIT from the requested output, omitting the $5,721.98 tax on $150,578.34 of net investment income. Its final $91,458 also contradicts its own regular-tax calculation of approximately $102,907.

The model fabricated mortgage interest from an assumed 7% rate and an unstated acquisition-debt history, despite the instruction that unlisted numeric inputs equal zero. It then assigned qualified dividends a 20% rate and produced a total unsupported by its component calculations.

The model incorrectly treated the 2026 tax system as a post-TCJA reversion, applying obsolete 15%, 25%, 28%, and 33% ordinary brackets, restored miscellaneous deductions, personal AMT rules, and a $10,000 SALT cap. It also invented mortgage interest using a 7% rate, creating a spurious AMT liability instead of the traced $89,095.92 ordinary tax.

The model invented approximately $28,000 of mortgage interest from the mortgage balance even though no interest expense was listed. That unsupported itemized deduction replaced the applicable $33,269.77 taxable-income deduction and distorted taxable income and tax.

The model imposed a $131,247 alternative minimum tax instead of applying the ordinary-rate calculation that yields $89,095.92. The resulting AMT-based total overstates the liability even after correctly recognizing that NIIT belongs in the output.

The model incorrectly assumed the TCJA individual provisions expired for 2026 and applied uncapped SALT deductions, a mortgage-interest deduction, and personal exemptions. Those obsolete and unsupported deductions produced $88,016 of ordinary tax instead of $89,095.92.

The model used an unexplained $460,936 AGI that does not follow from the listed income or the traced $541,928.34 gross-income base and $5,163.84 above-the-line deduction. It therefore applied the brackets to the wrong taxable-income base.

The model used approximately $525,049 of AGI rather than the traced income and deduction sequence and added an AMT component that does not determine the requested liability. This displaced the correct $89,095.92 ordinary-tax computation.

The model applied a post-TCJA-sunset regime with full SALT deductibility and ordinary rates reaching 33%. The applicable 2026 deductions and brackets instead produce $503,494.75 of taxable income and $89,095.92 of ordinary tax.

The model subtracted employer-sponsored insurance premiums from wages in addition to retirement deductions, reducing wages to $370,323 and AGI to $525,049. It also applied personal exemptions and a 20% qualified-dividend rate, neither of which belongs in the traced computation.

The model used 2025 brackets and a $30,000 standard deduction for a 2026 calculation, and it omitted the $5,721.98 NIIT. Its $430,498 ordinary-income base consequently generated the wrong ordinary tax.

The submitted $21,735 is incompatible with the model's own acknowledgment that substantial ordinary income and preferential-rate investment income remain taxable. The correct components are $89,095.92 of ordinary tax, $11,688 of preferential-rate tax, and $5,721.98 of NIIT.

The model invoked unspecified itemized deductions, including real-estate taxes and medical expenses, without applying the actual deduction limits or showing a taxable-income calculation. The traced deductions leave $503,494.75 taxable, which cannot produce its $75,657 estimate under the applicable rates.

The model used an estimated $32,200 standard deduction plus a separate $1,070 charitable deduction and arrived at $505,148 taxable income rather than the traced $503,494.75. Applying its estimated deduction and rate inputs produced regular tax $528.98 above the traced total.

The answer implies that the model understated the ordinary-rate component after recognizing both preferential qualified-dividend tax and NIIT. The applicable brackets on $503,494.75 of taxable income yield $89,095.92 of ordinary tax, bringing the total to $106,505.90.

The model said it used itemized deductions but did not derive them from listed expenses, and its total exceeds the traced result by $632.10. The applicable taxable-income deductions are $33,269.77, producing $503,494.75 of taxable income before the three tax components are summed.

The model identified the correct categories but applied estimated deduction or bracket values, overstating the total by $871.10. Exact 2026 computation gives $89,095.92 of ordinary tax, $11,688 of qualified-dividend tax, and $5,721.98 of NIIT.

The model introduced a qualified-business-income deduction even though the household has no listed qualified business income. It also referenced mortgage interest and unspecified credits without listed inputs, so its $112,450 residual is built on deductions and adjustments the household does not have.

The model incorrectly applied a post-TCJA-sunset regime with Pease limitations, personal exemptions, restored miscellaneous deductions, and a controlling AMT. The applicable 2026 ordinary-tax calculation is $89,095.92, with no $125,199 tentative-AMT substitution.

The model deducted $13,369 of employer health premiums from the reported gross wages, even though the traced employment-income calculation reduces wages only to $388,350. It also restored miscellaneous itemized deductions and omitted the applicable $33,269.77 taxable-income deduction, producing the wrong ordinary-tax base.
The model supplied no numeric output or explanation for the requested variable.

The model deducted the full $46,308 of desired 401(k) contributions and used $538,418 AGI with a $32,200 standard deduction, rather than the traced gross-income and deduction sequence. That produced $506,218 taxable income instead of $503,494.75 and overstated ordinary tax.

The model treated the mortgage balance as mortgage-interest expense and claimed SALT, medical, charitable, retirement, and employee-business deductions more than offset income.
